title: Kolache
subtitle: Czech yeasted pastry buns with cream cheese or poppy seed filling
excerpt: Kolache are round, soft Czech yeasted buns with a central indentation filled with cream cheese sweetened with vanilla and sugar, or with a thick poppy seed paste. The dough is enriched with butter and egg and is soft enough to pull apart. They are a breakfast or mid-morning pastry, best eaten within a few hours of baking. The fillings are generous; almost as much filling as dough.
sourceNotes: Kolache are a staple of Czech and Slovak home baking and have become a regional speciality in Czech-settled parts of Texas, where Czech immigrants brought the recipe in the nineteenth century. The Czech word kolač means a round cake or pastry. Recipes appear throughout Czech and Moravian baking manuals from the nineteenth century. The cream cheese filling is the most common in Czech homes; the poppy seed version is a Central European tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat at 160 degrees C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dough can be shaped and refrigerated overnight. Fill and bake straight from the fridge, adding 3 to 5 minutes to the bake time.

glossaryTerms.definition: A yeasted dough that includes butter, eggs, sugar, and often milk in addition to flour and water. The fats and sugars slow fermentation and produce a tender, close crumb that stays soft longer than a lean dough. Classic examples include brioche, milk buns, kolache, and hot cross buns. Because enrichment impedes gluten development, enriched doughs require longer kneading or a longer fermentation time to build the structure needed to hold the filling.

content.text: ; buttery and slightly sweet, soft enough to press down with a thumb. The filling indentation is made just before baking; if made earlier it fills in during proving. Two fillings are given here: a cream cheese version and a poppy seed version. Make both if you want variety on one tray.

items.prepNote: ground in a spice grinder or food processor

content.text: Combine 450 g flour, 7 g yeast, 50 g sugar, and 6 g salt. Add 200 ml warm milk and the beaten egg. Mix to a rough dough, then knead for 6 minutes until smooth. Add 60 g softened butter in small pieces, kneading between each addition, until fully incorporated. The dough will be soft and slightly sticky. Cover and leave at room temperature for 60 minutes until doubled.

Cream cheese filling: beat 200 g cream cheese with 40 g sugar, 1 tsp vanilla, and 1 egg yolk until smooth. Poppy seed filling: simmer 100 g ground poppy seeds with 80 ml milk, 60 g sugar, and 0.5 tsp vanilla over medium heat for 4 to 5 minutes until thick. Cool both fillings completely.

Line two baking trays. Knock back the dough and divide into 12 pieces, each about 65 g. Shape each into a smooth ball and place on the lined trays, spacing well apart. Cover and prove for 25 to 30 minutes until noticeably puffed. Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Use two fingers or the base of a small glass to press a deep indentation (about 4 cm across) into each bun. Fill each hollow with a heaped teaspoon of filling. Brush the exposed dough edges with beaten egg. Bake for 16 to 18 minutes until golden. Cool slightly on a rack before eating.

items.solution: The indentation was not deep enough, or the filling was too thin. Press the hollow deep enough to hold the filling; it will spring back a little as the bun bakes around it. If the cream cheese filling is very loose, add an extra egg yolk and refrigerate for 10 minutes before using.
They were overbaked, or cooled uncovered. Kolache stale quickly. Eat within a few hours of baking, or store in an airtight container and reheat briefly in the oven before serving.

The milk was too hot and killed the yeast, or the room was too cold for fermentation. Milk should be warm to the touch — about 35 degrees C — not scalding. In a cold kitchen, prove in an oven with just the light on.
